speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Erna Florsheim. October 20th, 1996. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were talking about the conditions in Rivesaltes and how sick your-- both your parents became. I wanted to go back, if I might, and just ask you, do you know why you went from Gurs to Rivesaltes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why they took us there, because not everyone in Gurs went to Rivesaltes. Mostly they took people with children to Rivesaltes. The reason why, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the they who-- whom you were aware of who took you or who made the decisions in either of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe the French were still in-- in power at that time. The Germans had not arrived as yet, because every morning, some French women came to the barrack. And they were counting to see if anyone escaped during the night. This is as far as I know. If it had something to do with the Vichy government or so, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people who gave you the bread and the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the-- from the same people, the same women that counted us in the morning. There was some-- there was one area. And some people from our people that were deported took care of cooking that soup, whatever they supplied-- these people supplied. And the bread that came in and-- each loaf was counted. And then it had to be cut in so many pieces. And I don't know where. I guess it-- I would assume it came from the government, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did the measuring of the bread, and the distribution of it, and the cutting of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each barrack had a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a leader and-- who took charge of these things. And with-- they were just selected at random. And maybe for one week, one person did it. For another week, another person did it. This is how it was done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- your mother ever do it in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she did not. She did-- she was not well enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it considered an honor or-- to do it, or just something that had to be done? Or was it considered odious to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I believe the people who did it were honored to be selected-- that everyone trusted them, and that they did-- that they were fair to everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were they selected? Did you all get together in some way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- the-- the people one-- in the evening, one evening, they said, all right. Tomorrow, we change whoever is gonna be in charge. Who wants to be in charge? And so on. And there was usually someone that wanted to do it. And there was-- there was no quarrel about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew one another well enough to have an idea of who would be honest and who would take better care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was from your daily living--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me some idea of how you came to have an idea of whom you could trust? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's difficult to say, because sometimes you meet people, you just feel comfortable with them. And this-- and I suppose the people in the barracks felt that way. I had no say so as a child. But through the years, I found by myself that there are people I can become close to and-- not confide in, but trust more and-- than some other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in those unusual circumstances, do you feel that the same-- the same ways we have of trusting were being used, or do you feel that anything else might have been necessary? Not-- perhaps I didn't say that very clearly. In the unusual circumstances, could you trust the methods that you had used, and-- and that you use now, for judging another human being?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think this is something that is in-- within you. This is not something that you learn. You do learn, yes, through experience in life. But my mother was very good in judging a person's character. And I believe some of it I inherited from her. I was with her for more years than my sister or my brothers. I was-- so I-- I took a lot of her ideas, and her feelings, and so on with me all my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: During these times that you were with her alone, did you speak a lot to one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: At times. At times. At times, it was difficult. It was difficult for her. It was difficult for me. She felt very bad for me, for the-- you know. As a matter of fact, my-- one of my uncles that survived-- he died in Israel-- he showed me a postcard that my mother wrote to him from camp-- from Gurs, as a matter of fact. And I read the card and-- where she said that she feels so bad for me to have a-- a childhood like I do. But on the other hand, she feels it's-- it's-- it's like a blessing that I'm with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm going to backtrack a bit. Could you tell me a bit about the train ride? Did you go on a train from Gurs to Rivesaltes? Is that how--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went by-- they put us on a truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long it took. I don't remember anymore how long it took for us to get there. But it was trucks that they had. They put us on, and they told us they were gonna take us to a different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your understanding of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why that might be?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea. We just had to go along with everything they said and everything that they did. We had no choice. We had-- we just went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father, and your mother, and you went. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were there any other people whom you knew well, who also went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Margot and her parents, and my uncle and aunt and their little daughter. And there was another aunt. She's a-- a widow. She was a widow. She was-- her husband was a brother of my mother, but I never knew him because he died of cancer very young. And she was with us also. And of course, all the people I went to school with-- or a lot of them, I should say, the majority-- who were still in Pforzheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those days, did you ever have occasion to smile, or to laugh, or to feel good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- very little. Very little, because it was always-- my-- my whole life, it was serious. There were no, as I said-- no childhood, no youth, and nothing. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the first impression that you had of Rivesaltes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first impression was I felt better. The sun was shining, number one. There was no mud. And there were little-- there's stones all over, but that didn't matter. It was cleaner-- a little bit cleaner than the other camp. It was not so overcrowded. And-- but the-- the circumstances as far to food and [? so ?] were the same as in Gurs. And-- and a lot of people died there of dysentery. There were no facilities. And my aunt-- her father-- he was quite old-- who was there with my uncle and my-- their little girl, he died there. And he-- also, a lot of people that I knew that-- died of sicknesses. There-- there was no medical help or facility.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that your uncle had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: That your uncle had died? How did you find out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which--

speaker: interviewer
text: That your uncle who died in Rivesaltes-- how did you find out that he had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, not my uncle. That was my aunt's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not-- it was the other side of the family that-- I was not really related to him. And he died. We were still-- we were there. We were, I mean, together. And I remember going even to the funeral because my mother was sick and she could not go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to have funerals?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- sort of, you know? They had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a cemetery where they took the people and-- because what else could they do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right within the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was outside the camp. I don't know if it was a Jewish cemetery. I don't. That I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were permitted to go outside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only with a permit. And as I mentioned before, my father was in the hospital in Perpignan. And this was outside the camp. And I-- my mother got a permit for me and-- to go, because there, you had to go by train. So since my mother couldn't come along, someone from the-- our barrack went with me. And I wanted to visit my father because he was very sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before the-- we went on the train, there was, like, a Gestapo or some-- there was someone there with guns walking around, and asked for our papers. And of course, I showed him the laissez-passer-- the-- that I could go. It was-- so he asked me my age. And he said I had to be-- I don't recall. Was it 17 or 18?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, that woman who was with me went-- had to go back to camp. And he took me to prison. And I was there with gypsies. And I was there for a long time. I mean, not a long time. It was a few weeks. My mother had went wherever she could to-- to get me out of there-- from there. And they-- after I finally did get out, because there was a rabbi, who was a rabbi in Germany. He-- he talked to the guards and all this, and that I was a young girl, and I should not be in there with these people, and-- whatever they said, I do not-- eventually, I-- they did take me out. And after a while, my father came. They sent my father back to Rivesaltes. And then my father heard of a-- a-- a place. Someone told him that he was going-- there was a place that-- where he could work outside the camp. It was a salt mine. It was quite a ways away from the camp. And if my father would join him. And so my father felt maybe he could help my mother and me by sending us a few potatoes, some food, or anything. So he decided to go. And he went with that man. [SOBBING] And that was the last time I saw my father. [CRYING] This was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and after a while, the Jewish organization in Paris-- it's called the OSE. And they tried to rescue-- rescue children, as many as they could, put them in homes and so on, because at that time the transports had started already for that-- for Auschwitz and the various camps. And actually, I was already over age. I-- but I was small in stature. So they took me out. And this-- out of Rivesaltes. And they took me to a Girl Guides home for-- for the summer. It was a very short time. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, before we go on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to that part of your life, a few questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us when, exactly, or how long you were in Rivesaltes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you left, did your mother remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did. As a matter of fact, I left with Rachel Hirsch. And I remember we had to walk to Perpignan, but we had to walk there through the fields with someone that guided us. And I remember my mother and Mrs. Hirsch standing there, waving to us. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] I wore a pair of shoes of-- from someone-- I don't know whose-- because my [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were imprisoned with the gypsies for those few weeks, was it all women?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there children there also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No children. No. They were all quite young, these women. And they didn't bother me. They didn't-- I don't even recall if they spoke to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you about 15 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 15, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn any terms you had never heard before? Any kind of slang, or expressions, or words?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I did, I didn't understand it. I don't know, because in those days, I didn't understand any French yet. And I-- nobody spoke German there. So I-- I don't-- if they did, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the gypsies treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very indifferent. As I said, they didn't bother much with me at all, and-- which was just as well. I was afraid, though. I was very frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were the sleeping arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was just cement-- cement floor, on the-- the cement floor. It was a barrack like any other where we lived. But just nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spend your days there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just sat there. There was nothing to read or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always hoping that my mother would come. And once-- there was a very high window. And I was climbing up a little bit on something. And I-- my mother was standing outside. And she was waving to me. And so I knew she-- you know, she tried everything. And soon after that, I-- they did let me go. They didn't say anything. And to this day, I don't know what that man thought where I was going, because he knew I was going to see my father. And I could-- I-- it's one thing I must say. I'm not the type of person who wishes someone bad. But I-- I was-- I really wished him only the bad things that what happened to him during the war, because there was no reason for him to do that to me. This is how I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to directly communicate with anyone at all during-- during that imprisonment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. As I said, only that wave to my mother outside. Otherwise, not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you felt that somehow, it would be taken care of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow. My mother, she-- she smiled at me and she-- like an indication that, you know-- that eventually, you know, somehow, something will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the gypsies share anything at all with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what your father was ill with when he was in Perpignan, in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had some infection in his mouth or something. I don't know whether it was blood poisoning or something. I don't know. It must have been very severe for them-- for the-- to take him there for-- the-- the authorities, you know? Otherwise, they would never let anyone get outside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both your mother and you had these shots that you mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was against diphtheria or something like this. But it was very painful. That I remember very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's infection--

speaker: interviewee
text: It--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how long did that take to heal?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took-- it took a long time, because there was no penicillin or this type of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your father went to work in the salt mine--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did your mother try and find out-- was there any way to try and look for him or find out what had happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew where he was, because he wrote to us postcards and-- from that place where he worked. And he did-- once or twice, he sent something to eat. It-- it was like oatmeal or something. And-- but we had no facilities to cook or-- there was nothing. So it was of no use, really, to us. But when-- after my mother-- after I left, my mother was alone in the Rivesaltes, and my father had-- was not there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She used to write to me through wherever I- she knew-- I always wrote to her a postcard or something where I was. And so once she wrote to me a postcard that she's waiting for my father to camp, because they transferred my mother from Perpignan to the Drancy. That is a camp-- the last camp before Auschwitz. And she thought that my father would come there, and they probably would be together. But from what I understand from someone that had some information from France, in a book, that they were-- went to Auschwitz on different days-- dates. The correspondence that I had both from my father and mother were taken away from me when I was in Vic-sur-Cere. And I had to leave there. And they buried it in the garden. They said after the war, I should come and pick it up. Of course, I never received it anymore. And that I regret very much, not having those last correspondence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Do you have any way of knowing if your mother ever saw your father again after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. And as I said, according to that information that I received, they did not see each other anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were still together with your mother, what kind of things would your father say in the postcards?

speaker: interviewee
text: He only wanted to know if we are well. They had to be very careful what they wrote to each other because everything was censored. So there was nothing of consequence that he could ask, or tell us, or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your last memory of being with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: When that-- in-- when that truck came, and that man my father went-- I can see so clearly in front-- in front of me. He was-- and he waved. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So OSE intervened, even though you were a little older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they took me out. And I went to that Girl Guides camp for a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Saint-Sorlin. That was in-- in the country. It was a very pretty surroundings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what year that was, or the approximate date?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] That was July 15, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Rivesaltes, then, before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I was from March 1941 to July 1942. From-- I-- yes, from-- until July '42, I was in Rivesaltes. And then I went to Saint-Sorlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember when you first arrived there? Were you-- when you left your mother, did you feel that you were in better hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- there was one couple that ran that Girl Guides camp. And Rachel Hirsch and myself, we were the only Jewish children there, because we were the only one that they took out from camp and took there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went together--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. And the couple that took care of the camp were Jewish, because they had to also leave their home in Nimes. They used to live in Nimes. And they were rounding up the French Jews also. So they did that to save themselves. And the OSE asked them to take care of the two of us and-- which they did. They were very kind to us. They were-- the first thing they did, they fed us. And they cleaned us-- they cleaned us up. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I wanted that was bread and potatoes. That's all I wanted. And to this day, this is my favorite food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the bath like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bath?

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they cleaned you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. It was a shower, a stand-up shower. It-- but it had hot water that we did not have in all that time. It was like being born again. It was something-- it's-- you cannot even put into words, the feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the clothing that you had worn?

speaker: interviewee
text: They burned it because we had body lice, head lice. We-- they burned it there. We couldn't-- and then they gave us some clothes. I don't remember. I suppose from one of-- from the girls, or maybe OSE provided it. I don't know, because I do know they-- the OSE paid for us whatever had to be taken care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the living accommodations like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Girl Guides?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a house. The other girls, they did have, like, bunkers or something. But that couple, they knew that we slept on cement floor for so long, they put us in the house in beds, and-- which was a luxury. This-- these feelings you never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you each have your own bed, you and Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. And we were in heaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you the only two children in that room with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so many. Maybe 15, 20. It was not a huge camp. It was just a-- you know, a small--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your daily life there like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yes. We were doing all-- all the things that the other young people did. The-- for the first time in our lives, we were with people approximately our age. We did craft, hand craft-- you know, craft work. We-- we were singing. They had-- like evenings, they had like a bonfire. And we were sitting around and singing and all this-- although we couldn't sing because we didn't know French. But it was nice. It was a-- it was like a different-- a different world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other children-- the non-Jewish children-- treat you any differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As a matter of fact, they were very helpful. Whether they were told about us something or so, I don't know. I have a feeling that they were told because they treated us with respect. They treated us nice. They did-- if they could be helpful in any way, they were. So I have a feeling that that couple must have spoken to them about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become close with anyone there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were close with Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Rachel, yes. But you see, that camp was only for a few weeks, because it closed. And then we were-- went to Vic-sur-Cere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just make sure I ask everything I'd like to know before we go on. While you were at the Girl Guide camp, were you able to communicate with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was, and also with my father. And this was-- the last postcards I received from them were there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I-- I think we'll-- we'll discuss that when we begin the next tape.
